chapters.title: A breakthrough that changed commerce
chapters.text:
In the mid 1980s, a powerful new idea took hold in business management. Building and growing a brand could be worth more than making the product itself. This marked a clear break from earlier decades, when production was the main measure of a company is strength.

During the Great Depression, for example, Fortune magazine stressed the value of making things and suggested that America is economic pain came from drifting away from production. The editorial argued that the "making of things" was the bedrock of an industrial economy, fueling both income and recovery.

That philosophy began to fade in the 1980s. Recession hit the world, and even strong manufacturers faced the hard truth that their huge operations, large workforces, and big assets had become a drag rather than an edge. The weight of running large scale production started to look like a liability, not an asset.

This era also saw the rise of companies like Nike and Microsoft, which took a different path. They claimed that production was a side issue. They used trade rules and labor reforms to move manufacturing to overseas contractors, and they poured energy into branding, the art of building an image for their products in the market.

The strategy paid off handsomely and kicked off a trend where the less a company owned its own production, the more successful it became. From then on, a corporation is strength was measured by the visibility and recognition of its brand, not by its factories and tools.

Many famous manufacturers no longer make their products. They buy and brand them. The focus is on creative marketing, brand extensions, and fresh imagery, not on factory equipment. When you pick a product, remember that big companies often care more about buzz than quality.

In this summary, we will look at how this obsession with brand identity has shaped society and how a new wave of anti corporate activism is pushing back.

chapters.title: Brands clever tricks for growth
chapters.text:
In our society, we are surrounded by brands. They show up on streets, at sporting events, in schools, and in malls. Our favorite teams and athletes are sponsored by brands, and most of what we see in movies and on TV is a carefully placed branded product.

Brands have drifted so far from their original job, which was to vouch for product quality, that they now exist mainly as emotional selling points. The reason brands loom so large in our culture comes from a special law of advertising gravity. The rule is simple. If you are not shooting upward, you will crash to the ground before you know it. Ad executive David Lubars puts it plainly. Consumers, he says, are like roaches. If you keep spraying them, they get resistant.

So if consumers are like roaches, then corporations and ad agencies must keep cooking up new mixes of high grade spray. That is what happened in the 1980s, when companies started tailoring their brand image to teenagers. Since brands must keep shifting to stay relevant, they had to invent a "cool" image so teens would spend their money on the products.

As ads grow more aggressive, we should push back and teach our kids to do the same. Companies would send scouts into high schools to learn what counted as cool and youth oriented, then turn it into ad campaigns. Nike and Tommy Hilfiger were the two big winners of this move, riding the hip hop and Black fashion trend from the start.

It is no secret that we now live a sponsored life, and with ad spending still rising, we can expect more of these tricks.

chapters.title: Megachains and their contradictions
chapters.text:
Despite the talk, market driven globalization has little to do with diversity. Local brands and small shops stand in the way of superbrands trying to take over the market. So what are the main tricks corporations use to push regular shops out, drown out rivals, and take control.

The first trend, Walmart is go to move, is price wars, where megachains deliberately undersell every competitor. The second, favored by Starbucks, is clustering, where they flood an area with so many chain stores that local cafes cannot survive.

As of early 2021, Walmart had 10,500 stores across 24 countries, selling a wide range of goods. Over 3,000 of them were Supercenters of around 180,000 square feet, packed with grocery and clothing shops, hair salons, pharmacies, and banks. Of the 10,500 stores, 599 were Sam is Clubs, members only stores that offer big discounts on bulk purchases.

Walmart is recipe for staying the world is biggest retailer by revenue has four steps. Build stores so large they tower over anything nearby. Demand the lowest possible prices from suppliers by promising huge orders. Set your prices so low that smaller stores cannot keep up. And cut costs further by placing hypermarkets next to warehouses.

All of this helps the megachain build a monopoly and crush local rivals, especially small retailers. Starbucks is method is different. It uses a clustering strategy, opening coffeehouses on nearly every corner of dense urban areas, leaving no room for local cafes. It is an aggressive strategy that "cannibalizes" sales from competitors. The company saturates the market until its own branches start taking customers from each other. Some stores will lose money, but the overall gain is huge. As giants like Starbucks, who can afford to take losses, grow, small businesses slowly disappear.

In short, clustering and price wars choke diversity and competition, the very values these huge multinationals claim to support. Before you open a business, check whether your field has room for rivals.

chapters.title: The hidden cost of mass production
chapters.text:
Nike co founder Phil Knight once said that production is worth little and that breakthroughs and marketing add the value. What Knight is really saying is that to his branded empire, production is nothing more than a dull, marginal chore. Multinationals like Levi is, Reebok, Walmart, and IBM now follow the "Nike Model" and skip production entirely. Rather than make the product, they outsource it.

Exploiting people and the environment should not be the price we pay for cheap goods. It is not just factory jobs that move offshore. The old idea that a manufacturer is responsible for its workers goes with them. Global brands prefer to hand the responsibility for production to their contractor. They tell the contractor what to make and how cheap it has to be so the brand can keep pouring money into marketing.

Products are made in the factory, but brands are made in the mind, as Walter Landor has put it. The focus on branding over making things is undeniably profitable, but someone still has to roll up their sleeves and produce the goods these companies sell under their logos. Usually, multinationals outsource their manufacturing to areas known as free trade zones, or Export Processing Zones.

Export Processing Zones exist in over 70 developing countries, employing more than 30 million workers. Wherever they are, the workers share the same grim story. They are forced to work more than 12 hours a day in miserable conditions. The work is low skill and tedious. The pay is tiny. The factories are run like the military, with abusive supervisors.

Most of these workers are young, vulnerable migrants drawn by the promise of steady work and good perks. The promise is hollow. These low paying jobs can vanish overnight if the big brand using the factory decides to move production elsewhere.

So rather than lifting developing economies, Export Processing Zones are simply a way for big brands to pad their profits by squeezing workers.

chapters.title: A broken promise of service work
chapters.text:
Even though multinationals have outsourced most of their manufacturing, they have not managed to get rid of the "burden" of salespeople. The service sector has become a much bigger source of jobs over the past four decades. The decline in manufacturing has pushed service jobs higher. Over 75 percent of US employment is now in services and retail.

When you look for a service job, double check what the employer actually promises. The story corporations push is that retail workers are somehow not real workers, so they do not deserve a living wage, job security, or benefits. Instead of helping their employees grow and support families, companies treat them as students looking for pocket money. The superstore and the mall now host a ballooning subcategory of joke jobs that are part time, unstable, and poorly paid.

The economy needs stable, well paid positions that adults can build a life on. Instead of full time roles with benefits, many workers are pushed into non contracted, poorly paid part time gigs with no real future.

Most of the big name brands in the service sector pay the legal minimum wage or just a little more, as Naomi Klein has noted. The main reason these corporations find it so hard to pay a living wage, Klein argues, is that they are obsessed with growth and expansion. They built their plans on dominating the market before their rivals do. They want to maximize profit no matter what, even if that means squeezing the workers who are a core part of the brand is image.

The result is a hollowed out service economy where the people who keep stores open cannot pay their rent, while the brand they serve posts record profits.

chapters.title: How activists hijack an ad campaign
chapters.text:
Naomi Klein says a wave of political activism is rising to stand up to big corporations. One of the main tools in this anti corporate movement is culture jamming. Culture jammers parody ads by hijacking billboards and rewriting the message. This group of activists believes that streets are public space, and the best way to fight back against corporations forcing us to look at images we never asked for is to counter the message behind those images.

Ridiculing the popular story can be one of the sharpest ways to weaken it. The main argument of jammers has been boosted by the recent rise in aggressive ads in public space. Ads are everywhere, painted and projected on sidewalks, wrapped around entire buildings and buses, placed in schools and basketball courts, and pushed across the internet. At the same time, jammers realized that many media channels sit in the hands of a few corporate giants, so their fight is also a fight for freedom of speech.

When culture jammers want to hit a corporation, they hack into its ads and send messages that contradict the original. By doing this, they force the company to fund its own boycott. The brand paid for the billboard, and any time people vandalize a logo, they tap into the huge resources spent to give that logo meaning.

There is no doubt this move is not enough on its own to break corporate power or soften the impact of branding, but it is a key part of anti corporate activism. The phrase culture jamming comes from radio jamming, which is the act of blocking signals to disrupt the flow of information.

chapters.title: The weak spot of big corporations
chapters.text:
It is not just the ads that are under attack. More activists around the world are teaming up to expose brand misconduct. The sole purpose of these new groups is to out the multinationals that benefit from oppressive government policies around the world.

For example, the Program on Corporations, Law and Democracy studies the history of corporations in the United States and works to weaken their grip. JUSTICE. DO IT NIKE is a group that calls Nike to account for its labor practices. And the Yellow Pages is a covert global network of hackers targeting the digital systems of big brands that separate human rights from trade deals with China.

According to Naomi Klein, most of the fights against big brands began in the mid 1990s, a period she calls the Year of the Sweatshop. Back then, a day rarely passed without Americans hearing fresh stories of the exploitative labor behind their favorite brands. A wave of journalism exposed the dependence of big names like Walmart, Nike, and Gap on sweatshop labor in third world countries.

Disney faced a crisis when reports surfaced of a supplier is shocking working conditions, so bad that workers had to feed their infants sugar water. An NBC investigation in December 1996 spread the scandal, using undercover footage to show the near enslavement of workers in Indonesian and Chinese factories.

All of these events led to the Year of Brand Attack, a period when many superbrands and their celebrity endorsers faced boycotts for their shameful labor practices and blatant disregard for human rights. The public should push corporations to care for both employees and the environment.

In short, as superbrands keep growing and weaving themselves into our daily lives, they face more scrutiny over their labor practices and international ties. Brand image, the main source of wealth for multinationals, turns out to be their weak spot.

chapters.title: In closing
chapters.text:
Logos are everywhere, from the streets to schools and even into our most private spaces. Big brands shape our cultural standards and tastes, and the aggressive marketing of multinationals has changed the old relationship between a producer and its product. Brands have drifted so far from their original job, which was to vouch for product quality, that they now exist mainly as selling points aimed at our emotions and mental space. Instead of selling products, corporations now market their brands.

The role of manufacturing in our economy has shrunk under this shift from product marketing to brand marketing. Multinationals have stepped away from production entirely and outsource it to offshore contractors. That move has let superbrands drop most of the responsibility that came with making things.

The brands not products strategy is profitable, but someone has to make the goods that carry these logos. The workers in free trade zones bear the heaviest cost, with long hours, miserable conditions, and very low pay.

The good news is that new activist groups are rising to challenge corporations. They push multinationals to take responsibility for their labor practices and to be more reasonable about ad placement. Thanks to the internet, people can come together from all over the world to build movements that challenge corporate power and reshape the influence of brands in our lives.

You can play a part. Check whether your favorite brand follows ethical labor practices. Send the company an email or call its hotline as a customer and share your concerns. Ask specific questions about how it treats its workers and demand fair treatment on their behalf.
